quality_notes: State-level literacy package has a credible recent evaluation and a clear official implementation source but still bundles several mechanisms rather than isolating one lever.
This is a research and policy movement rather than a single intervention so claims should stay descriptive unless tied to specific state implementations.
Bleiberg finds positive initial math effects with meaningful heterogeneity and unequal subgroup benefits, so this should be treated as mixed rather than uniformly successful.
Open impact report indicates stronger persistence completion and STEM outcomes but the program combines scholarship and support components and is not a randomized design.
Policy family has supportive evidence from Texas and Mississippi-related work but outcomes vary by design and there are known tradeoffs around retention and implementation.
National quasi-experimental evidence shows stronger math gains than reading gains with larger benefits for lower-achieving and disadvantaged students.
The Chicago studies provide strong quasi-experimental evidence but also document test-specific gains strategic behavior and uneven benefits across grades and interventions.

Florida A+ shows positive accountability effects especially in math but the mechanism appears tied more to stigma and pressure than to a clean policy channel and test-specific responses remain important.
MDRC long-run randomized evidence is unusually strong for a secondary career-pathway model and shows earnings gains without academic harm.
MDRC lottery-based evidence is strong on internships dual enrollment and associate degree completion though implementation models vary across sites and states.
Current open sources document program design and participant pathways better than causal impact so this should remain a promising but lightly evidenced apprenticeship model.
Randomized and quasi-experimental evidence suggests some academic and science benefits but effects are selective by grade and outcome rather than broad universal gains.
Recent Florida evidence shows delayed academic gains and lower absences but also short-run discipline costs and the broader literature remains contested.
